Name: adaptive_kv_cache_optimization
Title: Adaptive KV-Cache Optimization for Latency Reduction in GPU-Accelerated Inference
Short Hypothesis: By dynamically adjusting the KV-cache layout and retrieval strategies based on real-time profiling metrics, we can significantly reduce latency and improve throughput for transformer-based models during inference.
Related Work: Existing literature primarily focuses on static KV-cache implementations, with recent advancements in CUDA optimizing memory accesses. However, none have explored adaptive strategies that leverage real-time profiling to adjust cache layouts dynamically. This proposal seeks to fill that gap by implementing a novel adaptive mechanism that reacts to the current workload and system performance.
Abstract: Transformer-based models are revolutionizing natural language processing tasks, yet their inference times remain constrained by inefficient key-value (KV) caching mechanisms. This proposal introduces an innovative approach to optimize KV-cache layouts and retrieval strategies dynamically based on real-time performance profiling. By utilizing kernel-level throughput measurements, we will design an adaptive KV-cache system that modifies its structure to enhance memory access patterns and reduce latency. Our experiments will involve profiling existing KV-cache implementations while varying input sequences and task loads. We aim to demonstrate that our adaptive system can outperform static implementations by a measurable margin, assessed through wall-clock latency and tokens-per-second metrics on commodity GPUs. This work not only contributes to more efficient transformer inference but also establishes a framework for real-time adaptive optimization in machine learning deployments.
Experiments: 1. Implement a baseline KV-cache retrieval strategy and measure its performance (latency and throughput) on a commodity GPU using standard benchmarks.

2. Introduce an adaptive KV-cache mechanism that adjusts cache layouts based on runtime profiling metrics (e.g., cache hit/miss rates, memory bandwidth utilization) and compare its performance against the baseline.
3. Conduct A/B latency races under different input sequences and load conditions, reporting p50/p99 latencies and throughput under varying batch sizes.
4. Measure energy consumption per token for both the baseline and adaptive systems to evaluate efficiency.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The adaptive mechanism may introduce overhead that could negate latency gains if not carefully managed.
Dynamic profiling might require additional resources in terms of computation and memory bandwidth, especially under heavy loads.
The performance improvements might vary significantly across different models and workloads, making generalization challenging.
